local_names.romanized_local: Gu wu yin liao (Chinese); Minuman bijirin (Malay); Tāniya pāṉam (Tamil); Anāj pey (Hindi)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03301737 'cereal beverage' is the closest available class; no exact powdered cereal drink subclass exists in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0EJL 'Cereal-based beverages' is the closest defensible code; no powdered-format subcode exists.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept identified for powdered cereal drink as of 2024.

preparation_method: mixed with hot or cold water

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 200 ml of prepared cereal drink, made by dissolving roughly 25–30 g of powder in hot or warm water.

nutrient_source.title: Cereal drink — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from HPB Foodo entry for 'Cereal drink' (D04010001), a powdered grain-based beverage. All per-100g values preserved from the canonical HPB record.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate based on oat-based cereal drink / mixed grain beverage GI range 60–70
gi_source.source_match_notes: No exact GI entry exists for this specific powdered cereal drink formulation. GI of 65 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entries for oat-based and mixed-grain cereal beverages, which cluster in the GI 60–70 range (medium). Glycemic load calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (65 × 20.92) / 100 ≈ 13.6, using a 200 g serving containing approximately 20.92 g carbohydrate.

hypertension.key_factor: Added sugar content and sweetened instant drink mix classification
hypertension.guidance: Although sodium is low at 37 mg/100g, this sweetened cereal drink contains added sugar (4.25 g/100g) which drives insulin-mediated sodium retention; individuals with hypertension should choose unsweetened cereal drink variants or plain grain-based beverages without added sugar.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with added sugar; medium glycemic index (estimated 65)
type2_diabetes.guidance: This powdered cereal drink contains added sugar and has an estimated medium GI of 65, making it unsuitable for routine consumption by individuals with type 2 diabetes; unsweetened cereal drink formulations or plain oat-based beverages without added sugar are preferred alternatives.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Added sugar and unknown saturated fat content from potential palm-based creamer components
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The added sugar content (4.25 g/100g) and possible palm-based creamer ingredients in some formulations may contribute to dyslipidaemia; individuals with cardiovascular disease should select unsweetened, low-saturated-fat cereal drink variants and verify ingredient labels for palm oil or hydrogenated fat.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Added sugar with no meaningful anti-inflammatory offset
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: This sweetened cereal drink provides no meaningful anti-inflammatory nutrients (no omega-3, negligible polyphenols, low fibre at 1.03 g/100g); added sugar contributes to pro-inflammatory mediator production and individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should replace it with unsweetened green tea or plain oat milk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cereal drink (powdered) is a processed grain-based beverage widely consumed in Singapore and Southeast Asia, made by dissolving a powdered cereal mix in hot or warm water. It is commonly marketed as a nutritious and convenient breakfast or morning drink, and is available in supermarkets and provision shops across Singapore under various brand names. The powder is typically formulated from a blend of cereal grains such as oats, wheat, barley, or mixed grains, combined with sugar and sometimes a palm-based creamer. Known in Chinese as 谷物饮料 (gu wu yin liao) and in Malay as minuman bijirin, the drink has a mild, slightly sweet, toasty grain flavour with a smooth, lightly viscous texture when prepared. Per 100 g of prepared drink, it provides 60 kcal, 1.24 g protein, 1.44 g fat, 10.46 g carbohydrate, 4.25 g sugar, 1.03 g dietary fibre, 37 mg sodium, 30 mg calcium, and 0.54 mg iron. The added sugar content classifies this as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) under ADA and clinical nutrition guidelines, making it unsuitable for routine consumption by individuals with type 2 diabetes or hypertension without modification. The low sodium level (37 mg/100g) is a positive attribute, but the sugar-driven insulin response offsets this benefit for hypertensive individuals. The estimated glycemic index is 65 (medium), yielding a glycemic load of approximately 13.6 per 200 ml serving. Unsweetened variants are clinically preferable for metabolic health management.
common_user_queries: Is cereal drink good for high blood pressure?
What is the GI of cereal drink?
Is cereal drink suitable for weight loss?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from the canonical HPB Singapore Foodo database entry D04010001 with confidence 0.95. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for similar grain-based beverages and is documented as such. Multilingual names for Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi are standard translations of the food category name, consistent with Singapore's multilingual food labelling conventions.
notes: saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this powdered cereal drink are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry D04010001 as of 2026-04-16, and no supplementary data was available from USDA FoodData Central for an exact match. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for oat-based and mixed-grain cereal beverages (GI range 60–70); no exact GI entry exists for this specific product formulation. This food is classified as a generic record (not a branded product); actual GI may vary by formulation. Multilingual names (Chinese: 谷物饮料 / 穀物飲料; Malay: Minuman bijirin; Tamil: தானிய பானம்; Hindi: अनाज पेय) represent standard category-level translations consistent with Singapore's multilingual food environment and HPB food labelling conventions.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 3: Added mandatory Singaporean multilingual local names — Chinese Simplified (谷物饮料), Chinese Traditional (穀物飲料), Malay (Minuman bijirin), Tamil (தானிய பானம்), Hindi (अनाज पेय) — to local_names to satisfy G6 hard gate.
Iteration 3: Added all multilingual local name strings verbatim to flat aliases array and to aliases_by_language to satisfy Q8 and the aliases consistency rule.
Iteration 3: Added romanized forms (Gu wu yin liao) to aliases and aliases_by_language.romanized.
Iteration 3: Updated cuisine from 'Generic / International' to 'Singaporean / Southeast Asian' to reflect HPB source provenance and trigger correct multilingual treatment.
Iteration 3: Updated confidence_reason and notes to document multilingual name sourcing.
Iteration 3: Updated natural_language_description to include multilingual name references for LLM/RAG utility.
Iteration 2: Corrected ontology match types from 'approximate' to 'closest_parent'.
